summary: This brief post shares an article analyzing the comments found in leaked Windows NT/2000 source code. Atwood highlights the insight that self-deprecating comments like 'UGLY TERRIBLE HACK' are actually indicators of good code and conscientious developers, since poor codebases treat hacks as normal and unremarkable. The post suggests that all programmers under deadline pressure write comments they might regret, making it hypocritical to judge others for the same behavior.
key_points: Comments warning about 'ugly terrible hacks' indicate good code, not bad—because bad code treats hacks as unremarkable
All programmers under deadline pressure write comments they might later regret
Conscientious coders leave prominent warnings about tricky parts of code
Searching leaked code for embarrassing comments is hypocritical but entertaining
money_quotes: Comments like 'UGLY TERRIBLE HACK' tend to indicate good code rather than bad: in bad code ugly terrible hacks are considered par for the course.
In the struggle to meet deadlines, I think pretty much all programmers have put in comments they might later regret, including swearwords and acerbic comments about other code or requirements.
It would therefore be both hypocritical and meaningless to go through the comments looking for embarrassments. But also fun, so let's go.
key_insight: Self-critical comments in code are a sign of conscientious developers who recognize technical debt, not evidence of poor engineering.
